title: 2025 in Review: A Year of Enormous Changes at Sea
description: This issue features the winners of the 2025 General Prize Essay Contest. Special thanks to Andrew and Barbara Taylor for their enduring support for this contest.
text:
In May 1954, Samuel Huntington wrote what became one of the most important, widely read, and oft-cited Proceedings articles of all time: “National Policy and the Transoceanic Navy.” It argued for a large post World War II Navy focused on projecting power to the shores of the Eurasian rimlands. Huntington’s rubric lasted roughly 70 years—a period during which the U.S. Navy enjoyed control of the seas and did project power, with few hindrances, on Eurasia. But now, given the rise of Chinese sea power (writ large) and the resulting contest for control of the Pacific, Huntington’s era is over. Enter “National Policy and the Panoceanic Navy” by Navy Commander Jeff Vandenengel.
Using the same structure Huntington did 72 years ago, Commander Vandenengel describes the mission, policy, public support, and organizational changes the Navy needs now. We dedicated extra space to this article, and we encourage readers to give it the time it deserves and to debate it in wardrooms, ready rooms, fleet headquarters, and Pentagon and Capitol Hill offices.
Also in this Naval Review issue, we look back on 2025 across the Navy, Marine Corp, Coast Guard, Merchant Marine, and—new this year—NOAA. The authors of our review sections not only highlight the activities and headlines, but they also provide context.
“U.S. Navy Year in Review,” reminds us that 2025 brought a new service secretary and service chief, continued combat operations in the Red Sea, and a massive naval buildup in the Caribbean. “U.S. Marine Corps Year in Review” details the aggressive pace of the Marine Corps’ international exercises and Force Design developments—all of which occurred against the ongoing backdrop of too few amphibious ships. And the “U.S. Coast Guard Year in Review” likens 2025 to Star Trek: change at warp speed.
For the past 23 years, Dr. Shashi Kumar has summarized major events and changes in merchant shipping in the “Global Maritime Industry Year in Review.” For naval professionals, if you only read one article per year on the merchant shipping industry, this is the one to read.
And our new addition, the “NOAA Year in Review,” provides a look at the nation’s fifth Sea Service, the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration.
This issue also features the winners of the 2025 General Prize Essay Contest, our oldest and most prestigious. Special thanks to Andrew and Barbara Taylor for their enduring support for this contest.
Coast Guard Reserve Commander Kelsey Barrion took first prize with “Sealift: Tomorrow’s Fight Must Be Uncrewed but Not Unattended.” She makes the point that large uncrewed ships are required to support combat logistics in the western Pacific, but today’s uncrewed surface vessels (USVs) are not yet ready to operate autonomously for months at a time.
Navy Lieutenant Commander Jordan Spector won second prize with “UxS Priority One: Defend the Carrier,” which recommends the Navy focus its uncrewed system procurement and operational concepts in the near term on the vital need to defend aircraft carriers.
